name: engram_labeled_subset
description: A small hand-labeled query/relevant-doc set drawn from the assistant's own knowledge/memory domain (operations, agent behavior, design conventions, scheduling). Measures DOMAIN FIT for a candidate embedding model. PII-SWEPT: ships only abstracted text — no IP addresses, host or container identifiers, person names, email addresses, tokens, or secrets. Every doc is a generalized restatement of a real memory note with all infra values removed. The gap between this set and the public baseline is the calibration signal.

docs.text: The orchestrator agent never executes bulk work itself; it delegates to sub-agents and keeps its own tool calls to a small number per user request.
Routine messages such as budget alerts and maintenance reminders are produced from templates with variables, not generated by a language model, to keep cost near zero.
Every HTML page generated by the system links the shared stylesheet; hardcoded colors and inline layout styles are not allowed.
When deciding how to handle a task, prefer the standard library first, then a template, then a keyword lookup table, before reaching for any model inference.
Work-management API calls go through the rate-limited helper client, which enforces a minimum interval between calls and retries on throttling responses.
Each agent is described by a single definition file holding its display name, ordered model routing, allowed tool categories, and memory namespace.
Sub-agents are assigned the cheapest inference that still works for the task; inference is allocated per task rather than uniformly across the fleet.
Messages between agents are not sent peer to peer; everything routes through the lead orchestrator by way of the shared inbox system.
The smart proxy filters the full tool catalog down to a smaller relevant subset per turn using keyword categories, reducing the prompt the model must reason over.
A background process started with a detached shell command does not survive; long-running services must be defined as managed system units instead.
Secrets are never pasted into a conversation; they are fetched from the secrets manager or stubbed as placeholders and filled in afterward.
The briefing agent synthesizes many data sources into a single narrative, which is one of the few cases where language-model generation is genuinely warranted.
The monitoring agent watches operational health and raises an alert when a threshold such as budget used or commute delay is exceeded.
Renaming a tool module requires updating the server registration that imports and registers it, then testing before the old file is removed.
A scheduled routine that uses tools cannot be edited in place; it must be deleted and recreated to change its behavior.
The knowledge base stores journals, behavioral patterns, and notes as data, kept separate from the executable agent code.
Date and time arithmetic such as next-due dates, streak counts, and days remaining is computed in code, never delegated to a model.
A new agent is added by writing its definition file into the agents directory; the naming layer then picks it up automatically with no code change.
The work queue manager receives a work order, executes it, and returns the result back through the orchestrator to the operator.
Status filtering by group is unreliable in the work-management backend, so results are filtered on the client side instead.
Documentation for each module lives under the docs directory and is also surfaced through the admin panel's help endpoint.
The deep-reasoning council is reserved for critical architectural decisions and is gated behind explicit approval because of its cost.

queries.text: Should the lead agent do heavy work itself or hand it off?
How do we keep routine notification messages from costing money?
What styling rule applies to generated web pages?
What is the decision order before using model inference?
How should calls to the work-management system be made safely?
Where is an agent's routing and tool configuration defined?
How do agents communicate with each other?
How is the tool list kept small for each model turn?
Why doesn't a backgrounded command keep running, and what should I use?
How are credentials handled so they stay out of chat?
When is it actually worth using a language model to generate text?
What does the health-monitoring agent do?
What steps does renaming a tool module take?
Can a scheduled routine with tools be edited in place?
How do I add a brand-new agent to the fleet?
What does the work queue manager agent do end to end?
Why filter results on the client instead of by the backend?
Where does module documentation live and how is it served?
